content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance · · Administration · · Executive/Legal · · Enforcement
(916) 322-5662                322-5660              322-5901              322-6441


April 17, 1985


Ed Struiksma
City Councilman, Fifth District
City Administration Building
202 C Street
San Diego, CA  92101

RE:  Advice Request No. 85-091

Dear Mr. Struiksma:

     Chairman Stanford has asked me to respond to your letter of
April 2, 1985.  In your letter you asked whether corporations
may make certain payments in connection with a fundraising event
you are planning on the Viking Princess Cruise Ship.

     The Political Reform Act does not prohibit corporations from
making contributions or other types of payments in connection
with election campaigns.  Therefore under the state law, there
is no prohibition against these payments.  However, if they are
contributions, they must be reported on your committee's
campaign statement.  Your letter did not contain sufficient
facts to enable us to determine whether or not these payments
are contributions.  If you wish advice on this point, please let
us know.

     As you know, the San Diego ordinance, unlike the Political
Reform Act, does prohibit corporate contributions and certain
other contributions or payments.  We cannot advise you on how
the San Diego ordinance applies in the situation you described.
I suggest you contact the City Attorney in that regard.

Very truly yours,


Barbara A. Milman
General Counsel

BAM:nwm
cc: John Witt, San Diego City Attorney

THE CITY OF

SAN DIEGO

April 2, 1985

ED STRUIKSMA
COUNCILMAN
FIFTH DISTRICT

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804
ATTN: Dan Stanford

Dear Mr. Stanford:

My supporters have expressed a desire to conduct a
fundraising event aboard the Viking Princess Cruise Ship
owned by Crown Cruise Lines. The event would begin at
10:00 a.m. with the cruise ship embarking for Ensenada, with
a return to San Diego at 1:00 a.m. the following day.

A cocktail reception will be held on-board sometime
during the cruise. Cocktails will be no-host and will be
the responsibility of those attending the reception.

The campaign intends to ask for a contribution of $150
per person, payable to the Committee to Re-Elect Ed
Struiksma. Proceeds of the contribution will be used to pay
for mailing costs and printing of the invitations. There
will be no proceeds from the $150 contribution paid to Crown
Cruise Lines.

Corporations, businesses, or individuals will pay
separately, and directly to the Crown Cruise Lines for the
cruise passage and/or staterooms on board.

The question which requires a legal opinion is whether
corporations may pay for passage and staterooms directly to
the cruise line, while individuals who are attending the
fundraising reception on board are contributing separately
to the Committee to Re-Elect Ed Struiksma.

I would appreciate a response to this question at your
earliest possible convenience. The campaign will not
proceed until clearance has been received.

Sincerely,

Ed Struiksma

City Administration Building • 202 C Street • San Diego, California 92101 • (619) 236-6655
Treasurer's Office • 1200 Third Avenue, Suite 700 • San Diego, California 92101
Not Printed Or Mailed At Government Expense

sections.question: Whether corporations may make certain payments in connection with a fundraising event you are planning on the Viking Princess Cruise Ship.
sections.conclusion: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit corporations from making contributions or other types of payments in connection with election campaigns. Therefore under the state law, there is no prohibition against these payments. However, if they are contributions, they must be reported on your committee's campaign statement. Your letter did not contain sufficient facts to enable us to determine whether or not these payments are contributions. The San Diego ordinance, unlike the Political Reform Act, does prohibit corporate contributions and certain other contributions or payments, and the FPPC cannot advise on how the San Diego ordinance applies.
sections.question_synthetic: Can corporations pay directly to a cruise line for passage and staterooms in connection with a fundraising campaign event, while individuals separately contribute to the campaign committee?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Under California's Political Reform Act, corporations may make payments in connection with election campaigns, but if they constitute contributions, they must be reported. The FPPC cannot determine if the proposed payments are contributions without additional facts, and the applicant should consult the San Diego City Attorney regarding local ordinance restrictions on corporate contributions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both incoming request (dated April 2, 1985) and FPPC response (dated April 17, 1985). The question and conclusion sections were reconstructed from narrative text rather than labeled sections. The FPPC response appropriately defers to local counsel regarding San Diego ordinance compliance.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether corporations may make certain payments in connection with a fundraising event you are planning on the Viking Princess Cruise Ship.

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit corporations from making contributions or other types of payments in connection with election campaigns. Therefore under the state law, there is no prohibition against these payments. However, if they are contributions, they must be reported on your committee's campaign statement. Your letter did not contain sufficient facts to enable us to determine whether or not these payments are contributions. The San Diego ordinance, unlike the Political Reform Act, does prohibit corporate contributions and certain other contributions or payments, and the FPPC cannot advise on how the San Diego ordinance applies.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to City Councilman Struiksma's inquiry about whether corporations can pay for cruise passage and staterooms as part of a fundraising event. The response indicates state law allows corporate payments but requires reporting if they are contributions, while noting that San Diego's local ordinance may impose additional restrictions requiring consultation with the City Attorney.
